step_by_step_thinking: \boxed{A} Neurofilament light chain (NfL) is consistently described across multiple sources as a biomarker reflecting axonal damage and neurodegeneration. Its elevation is observed in conditions involving traumatic brain injury, spinal cord injury, motor neuron disease, frontotemporal dementia, chronic traumatic encephalopathy, and diffuse axonal injury—all of which are characterized by neurodegeneration. Moreover, its use as a measure of disease progression (e.g., reduced levels with effective therapy via tofersen) underscores its relevance as a dynamic indicator of neurodegenerative processes.
raw_response:
\boxed{A} Neurofilament light chain (NfL) is consistently described across multiple sources as a biomarker reflecting axonal damage and neurodegeneration. Its elevation is observed in conditions involving traumatic brain injury, spinal cord injury, motor neuron disease, frontotemporal dementia, chronic traumatic encephalopathy, and diffuse axonal injury—all of which are characterized by neurodegeneration. Moreover, its use as a measure of disease progression (e.g., reduced levels with effective therapy via tofersen) underscores its relevance as a dynamic indicator of neurodegenerative processes.
\boxed{A}
